questions.question: Define a leftist tree. Explain the s-value (shortest value) and the leftist property. Demonstrate the merge operation on two min-leftist trees with an example.
keyPoints: Leftist tree is a heap-ordered binary tree with the leftist property
s-value of a node = length of shortest path from node to null; s(null) = 0
Leftist property: for every node, s(left child) >= s(right child)
Merge: compare roots, recurse on right subtree of winner with the other tree
After recursion, swap children if leftist property is violated, update s-value
Show step-by-step merge example with s-values

questions.question: Explain insertion and deletion operations in a min-leftist tree. What is the time complexity of each operation and why?
keyPoints: Insert: create a single-node leftist tree, merge with existing tree; O(log n)
Delete-min: remove root, merge left and right subtrees; O(log n)
Both reduce to the merge operation
Rightmost path length <= log2(n+1), and merge follows right paths
This bounds all operations at O(log n)

questions.question: Compare leftist trees with binary heaps. Why are leftist trees preferred when frequent merge operations are required?
keyPoints: Binary heap merge: O(n) because heaps must be rebuilt
Leftist tree merge: O(log n) by following short right paths
Binary heap insert/delete: O(log n), same as leftist trees
Binary heaps are better for simple PQ operations due to array storage and cache efficiency
Leftist trees are preferred when merging two priority queues is a frequent operation
